eaip:Metadata.eaip:Title: Enterprise AI Agent Interoperability Protocol (EAIP) Technical Specification

eaip:Section.eaip:Title: 1. Necessity of Standardization
eaip:Section.eaip:Content: As enterprises deploy heterogeneous AI agents across diverse cloud and on-premise environments, the lack of a common communication protocol leads to silos, security vulnerabilities, and integration complexity. Standardization is necessary to ensure: - Seamless discovery and handoff between agents from different vendors. - Consistent security posture via cryptographically verifiable identities. - Traceability and auditability of state transitions across long-running agentic workflows. - Reduced operational overhead for managing multi-agent systems at scale.

eaip:Section.eaip:Title: 2. API Architecture
eaip:Section.eaip:Content: EAIP evaluates three primary transport patterns: REST, gRPC, and WebSockets.

eaip:Subsection.eaip:Title: 2.1 gRPC Mandate
eaip:Subsection.eaip:Content: For agent-to-agent communication, gRPC over HTTP/2 is MANDATED. gRPC provides superior performance through binary serialization (Protobuf), native support for bidirectional streaming, and strict schema-first contracts. REST is relegated to edge ingress, and WebSockets are restricted to legacy notification channels.

eaip:Section.eaip:Title: 3. IAM for Autonomous Agents
eaip:Section.eaip:Content: Autonomous agents require a zero-trust identity framework that does not rely on static API keys or credentials.

eaip:Subsection.eaip:Title: 3.1 SPIFFE/SPIRE Identity
eaip:Subsection.eaip:Content: EAIP utilizes the Secure Production Identity Framework for Everyone (SPIFFE) for workload identity. SPIRE serves as the reference control plane, issuing short-lived X.509 SVIDs (SPIFFE Verifiable Identity Documents) to agent workloads. This enables mutual TLS (mTLS) and automatic rotation of credentials without manual intervention.

eaip:Section.eaip:Title: 4. State & Error Management
eaip:Section.eaip:Content: State management in EAIP is handled via the Recursive Context Envelope (RCE).

eaip:Subsection.eaip:Title: 4.1 Recursive Context Envelope (RCE)
eaip:Subsection.eaip:Content: RCE provides a Merkle-tree structured state substrate. Every gRPC request MUST carry a root RCE at depth 0. As agents delegate tasks, they append nested child envelopes, ensuring a verifiable lineage of state transitions and decision logic.

eaip:Subsection.eaip:Title: 4.2 Error Recovery
eaip:Subsection.eaip:Content: Failures trigger an RCE rollback to the last committed checkpoint. EAIP leverages gRPC status codes (e.g., UNAVAILABLE, DEADLINE_EXCEEDED) with custom detail payloads for granular remediation.

eaip:Section.eaip:Title: 5. Conformance
eaip:Section.eaip:Content: Implementations must satisfy the mandatory requirements set forth in sections 2.1, 3.1, and 4.1 to claim basic conformance. Full conformance requires adherence to all normative sections including advanced federation and audit retention policies.

eaip:ReferenceArchitecture.eaip:Description: The EAIP Reference Architecture illustrates the flow from SVID acquisition to task execution and RCE state update.
eaip:ReferenceArchitecture.eaip:Diagram:
sequenceDiagram participant Caller as Caller Agent participant SPIRE as SPIRE Server participant Registry as Agent Registry participant Target as Target Agent participant RCE as RCE Store
Caller->>SPIRE: Request SVID SPIRE-->>Caller: X.509 SVID Caller->>Registry: Lookup Target(capabilities) Registry-->>Caller: Target Endpoint Caller->>RCE: Init Envelope Caller->>Target: HandoffRequest(SVID, State Token) Target->>RCE: Read Context Target->>Caller: TaskStream (Bidi) Target->>RCE: Update Context Target->>Caller: TaskComplete
